1|Introduction  
 
 
Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA) is a well-known way to check how well things work in managing and 
planning supply chains, used a lot for projects to keep things lasting. People often use DEA to measure how 
well supply chains do, like in the car-making business [1]. It's also used to see if outsourcing work in supply

Clustering with Machine Learning and Using NDEA 
in Development Planning: A Case Study in the 
Petrochemical Two-Stage SSC  
Sina Sayardoost Tabrizi1, Keikhosro Yakideh1,*
, Mahmoud Moradi1, Mostafa Ebrahimpour1   
 
1 Departmant of Industrial Management, Faculty of Management and Economics, University of Guilan, Rasht, Iran;

Accepted: 17 October 2024 
Sayardoost Tabrizi, S., Yakideh, K., Moradi, M., & Ebrahimpour, M. (2025). 
Clustering with machine learning and using NDEA in development planning: A 
case study in the petrochemical two-stage SSC. International journal of research in 
industrial engineering, 14(2), 355-384. 
Abstract 
This research utilizes Machine Learning (ML) to improve the evaluation of Decision-Making Units (DMUs) in a two-stage 
sustainable supply chain in the petrochemical industry in Iran. Efficiency calculations were conducted for 28 units over 90 
time periods. The inputs and outputs of the supply chain were selected according to sustainability criteria, facilitating 
accurate estimations for production planning and unit development through a hybrid proposed method utilizing both ML 
and Network Data Envelopment Analysis (NDEA). The goal is to use ML clustering methods alongside network NDEA 
models to determine the most effective clustering algorithms for categorizing homogeneous units. The primary goal of our 
research is to leverage ML techniques to enhance the accuracy of decision-making processes, specifically in the clustering 
of similar units, to assess efficiency. The main goal is to create strategies for improving the performance of inefficient units 
by comparing them to the most efficient units in each cluster. By implementing the Deep Embedded Clustering (DEC) 
algorithm, we have discovered substantial improvements in efficiency assessment and development planning. The contrast 
between clustering outcomes and the traditional NDEA model highlights the importance of clustering in assessing the 
proximity to the efficient frontier and enabling focused development strategies. This study emphasizes the significance of 
employing ML for clustering to improve efficiency evaluations and strategic planning for sustainable development in 
industrial facilities. The results showed that the use of clustering for assessing the relative efficiency of units compared to 
the non-clustering method with DEA could averagely reduce 18% of the distance of inefficient units from the cluster's 
efficiency frontier, representing a more attainable ideal goal for inefficient units. 
Keywords: Clustering, Machine learning, Sustainable supply chain management, Data envelopment analysis.

chains is good, looking at how companies perform and the limits in control and seeing what's happening [2]. 
DEA helps with finding problems and solutions, and understanding green supply chain work [3]. In keeping 
supply chains lasting, DEA is very important. Reports have used DEA to look at how lasting supply chains 
are, using moving network ideas [4]. DEA ways have been made to guess which group suppliers in lasting 
supply chains belong to, helping in picking which suppliers to use [5]. Also, new DEA ways have been put 
out to judge how lasting supply chains are, focusing on full and part work with wanted and unwanted results 
[6]. 
Assessing sustainability in Supply Chain Management (SCM) within industries is a critical area of knowledge 
that has seen significant advancements through the application of DEA. DEA has emerged as a powerful 
tool for evaluating different components of SCM, particularly in terms of sustainability. By introducing non-
radial DEA models, researchers have been able to assess the environmental efficiency of suppliers, 
contributing to the selection and evaluation process within SCM [7]. The concept of sustainability in SCM 
involves meeting a broader set of criteria, often referred to as the triple bottom line approach, which 
encompasses economic, environmental, and social aspects [8]. Studies have integrated DEA with stochastic 
double frontier analytic models to evaluate the sustainability and resilience of supply chains, considering 
factors like netzero and multidimensional features [9]. This integration allows for a comprehensive assessment 
of sustainability in global supply chains. 
DEA has also been utilized in the development of novel models, such as the inverse network dynamic DEA 
model, to evaluate the sustainability of supply chains by determining the point at which the Local Best 
Decision (LBD) occurs [10]. These models provide insights into the efficiency and sustainability of supply 
chain Decision-Making Units (DMUs) through dynamic network DEA approaches. In summary, the 
application of DEA in assessing sustainability in SCM has led to significant advancements in evaluating 
environmental efficiency, resilience, and overall sustainability performance within supply chains. By 
integrating DEA with various modeling approaches and frameworks, researchers have been able to provide 
valuable insights into enhancing sustainability practices in industries through effective SCM. 
Using closed-loop systems in the state-of-the-art model based on the Circular Economy (CE) concept and 
the efficiency assessment of DMUs using DEA is crucial for sustainable resource management. The CE 
emphasizes closed loops for materials, focusing on recovering and recycling to keep resources circulating 
within the economy [11]. This concept involves minimizing resource input and waste by closing material and 
energy loops. To implement CE strategies effectively, closed-loop thinking requires innovation and adaptation 
[12]. 
Undesirable outputs are a crucial component in the evaluation of efficiency using Network Data Envelopment 
Analysis (NDEA) models. Undesirable outputs are defined as outputs that have negative impacts on the 
system or environment. In the context of NDEA models, these outputs represent the negative consequences 
or by-products of production processes that must be minimized or eliminated to enhance overall efficiency. 
NDEA models are specifically designed to incorporate both desirable and undesirable outputs when assessing 
DMUs. This approach allows for a comprehensive evaluation of efficiency by considering the impact of both 
types of outputs on the performance of DMUs [13]. By integrating undesirable outputs into the analysis, 
NDEA models offer a more accurate depiction of efficiency, capturing the trade-offs between desirable and 
undesirable aspects of production processes [14]. 
The presence of undesirable outputs in NDEA models requires a balanced approach that focuses not only 
on maximizing desirable outputs but also on minimizing undesirable outputs to improve overall efficiency. 
By including undesirable outputs in the evaluation framework, NDEA models provide a holistic view of 
efficiency, considering the full spectrum of outputs generated by DMUs [15]. In conclusion, the incorporation 
of undesirable outputs in NDEA models is essential for a comprehensive assessment of efficiency and the 
promotion of sustainable practices. By accounting for both desirable and undesirable outputs, NDEA models 
offer valuable insights into the efficiency of DMUs, empowering organizations to make informed decisions 
to optimize their processes while mitigating negative impacts on the environment and society.

In countries with natural resources of oil and gas, the process of extracting crude oil and transporting it to 
the refineries is typically managed through a multi-level supply chain. In this study, a two-level supply chain 
model is proposed, consisting of the oil extraction stage (Stage 1) and the refining stage (Stage 2), and assessed 
using the efficiency evaluation model based on NDEA. Each petrochemical unit in the supply chain is 
considered a DMU in the network model, and the inputs and outputs at the beginning and end of the network, 
as well as the exchanges between the two levels of the supply chain, are all taken into consideration when 
determining the efficiency [16]. 
Improving operational efficiency and fairly evaluating decision units present significant obstacles in SCM . To 
overcome these hurdles, it is crucial to categorize similar and comparable units into clusters and then perform 
DEA calculations for each cluster individually. This approach allows the most efficient unit within each cluster 
to function as an exemplary model for the inefficient units. On the other hand, the most efficient unit of each 
cluster will be able to move to the higher cluster and be ready to compete to improve its efficiency and 
sustainability with its decision units in future periods. The use of clustering methods was placed on the agenda 
[17]. In similar research using an artificial neural network, decision units were placed in homogeneous clusters 
and efficiency calculation was evaluated using efficient and inefficient boundary methods [18]. Utilizing the 
applications of artificial Neural Networks (NN) is not merely subject to clustering, as in the paper [19]; it has 
been used alongside dynamic and network envelopment analysis to predict sustainability in the future. Using 
artificial intelligence to predict efficiency to provide a solution for over-inefficiency improvement is one of 
the most state-of-the-art methods in the field of sustainable supply chains. Our novelty is using Machine 
Learning (ML) for clustering two-stage SSC of the petrochemical industry in Iran for the first time and 
proposing a new method for assessing efficiency with NDEA. This section can also be named as one of the 
knowledge gaps identified in this research. In summary, the contributions of the study are as follows: 
I. We proposed a hybrid approach with NDEA and ML for clustering the supply chain of petrochemical 
industries. 
II. We compared the efficiency of the two-stage sustainable supply chain in the presence of undesirable output 
and without the presence of them. 
III. We provide a new comparable clustering method to find the best clustering algorithms for our dataset. 
IV. For the first time, we investigated the impact of clustering with ML algorithms on calculating the efficiency 
of SSC to provide the improvement solution and development plan for inefficient DMUs. 
The rest of this paper is organized as follows. A literature review is presented in Section 2. In Section 3, the 
proposed method is given. A case study is presented in Section 4. Finally, managerial implications and 
concluding remarks are discussed in Sections 5 and 6. 
2|Literature Review 
Articles related to this research are divided into three main sections. According to the title of the paper, the 
main priority was to calculate the efficiency of grouping DMUs into homogeneous clusters. Therefore, the 
main center focused on the study of algorithms in the context of ML After completing the clustering using 
network data enveloping analysis, inputs and outputs were examined sustainable approach in the two-stage 
supply chain of the petrochemical industry in Iran. In this way, for main parts of the research background 
are: 1) the application of ML in SCM, 2) the sustainability of supply chains, 3) network envelopment analysis, 
and 4) the knowledge gap. 
2.1|Application of Machine Learning in Supply Chain Management 
AI offers many benefits in operations and SCM, such as improved productivity, reduced errors, improved 
processes, and increased productivity. AI has a significant impact on business processes through real-time 
analysis, predictive maintenance and quality control. Implementation challenges include identifying needed 
capabilities, addressing cost issues, and navigating potential barriers to successful AI integration. In spite of

2.2|Sustainability of Supply Chains 
The supply chain has a complex multi-layered network. On the other hand, supply chains consist of 
departments or organizations that produce and distribute goods to end customers. Adding value to all 
stakeholders involved in the supply chain is the ultimate goal of every company. In evaluating the efficiency 
of a DMU, it is important to address various steps. Sustainability of the supply chain includes economic 
benefits, social responsibility, and environmental protection [22]. Sustainability implies a fine balance between 
economic benefits, environmental protection, and institutional social responsibility. In order to be sustainable, 
companies strive to produce environmentally and socially friendly products. Furthermore, all sustainability 
standards should be considered sustainable. Sustainability plays an important role in achieving sustainable 
supply chains [23]. 
The SSCM refers to the consideration of environmental, social, and economic criteria when evaluating supply 
chains [24]. In recent years, sustainability has become an essential part of SCM. Focusing on greener products 
and services in the SCM is a response to the pressures from authorities, environmentalists, customers, and 
Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs). Therefore, communities, businesses, and NGOs are looking for 
tools to measure the sustainability progress of organizations [25]. In [25], authors conducted a literature review 
on SSCM and discussed possible implications for future research works. Furthermore, sustainability is a key 
issue for researchers because of the decline in natural resources and concerns regarding capital disparity and 
societal obligations [26].  
The [27] discussed that concerns about sustainability have increased the accountability and responsibility of 
supply chains. They proved a significant relationship between the various aspects of sustainability and firm 
performance. Recently, Alrasheedi et al. [28] suggested a hybrid fuzzy decision-making framework to assess 
the sustainability of suppliers in manufacturing firms. Furthermore, in an attempt to justify the 
implementation of Blockchain,  Yadav and Singh [29] developed an integrated fuzzy-ANP framework for 
assessing sustainable supply chains. The [25] conducted a literature review on SSCM and discussed possible 
implications for future research works. In [27], authors used the SSCM concept to evaluate sustainable 
suppliers via the congestion approach of the economies of scale theory of DEA. Other related papers 
improved the SSCM using a hierarchical grey-Decision Making Trial and Evaluation Laboratory

(DEMATEL) approach. DEMATEL is an applicable tool used for assessing interdependent interactions 
among factors to identify cause-effect chain components of a complicated network [30].  
Organizations are responsible for their environmental and social performance. Sustainable development 
emphasizes on environment, economy, and society. Evaluating Sustainable Supply Chain Management 
(SSCM) performance is a key challenge. SSCM has turned out to be a strategic process that enables firms to 
create a competitive advantage. Customers' demands and products' complexity have increased competition 
among organizations. SSCM provides an opportunity for organizations to distinguish itself from its 
competitors. Thus, it provides a competitive advantage in the market. Numerous organizations have already 
commenced to develop a certain level of commitment toward sustainability practices to make their supply 
chains sustainable. SSCM evaluation, hence, is a substantial duty for any organization. Among evaluation 
methods, DEA appears to be a suitable technique to assess SSCM. 
2.3|Network Data Envelopment Analysis 
Today, due to the global shape of markets and increasing competition between supply chains, paying attention 
to sustainability criteria is an inevitable task for all businesses. A two-stage DEA network model was proposed 
in [31] for assessing Sustainability Efficiency (SE) and Profitability Efficiency (PE) of Mining Multinational 
Enterprises (MNEs). Higher sustainability is associated with increased returns for companies, showing that 
improvements in SE can lead to better PE. A framework was introduced in this method besides the multistage 
model with NDEA. Our purpose is to use DEA in the petrochemical industry to measure efficiency with 
DEA to evaluate the performance of multistage units in the petrochemical industry supply chain in Iran. This 
model allows for a more comprehensive analysis of efficiency within the supply chain of petrochemical 
companies. By considering the interconnectedness of multistage units, the NDEA model offers a more 
accurate evaluation of overall efficiency and the efficiency of individual components. 
The primary goal of [32] is to provide an inverse DEA model for a two-stage network-structured production 
system in the presence of undesired factors with weak disposability, given the significance of network-
structured production systems in real life. The suggested model's main goal is to estimate the amount that 
one or more process indicators would change if another stage's indicators were changed while maintaining 
the same degree of efficiency. The ability of the suggested procedure to simultaneously lower input levels and 
raise output levels is by far its greatest benefit. 
However, one of the most growing criticisms in these sorts of models is that the DMUs are assessed in a 
specific period. Given fluctuations in the performance of DMUs during several periods, considering just a 
specific period is insufficient in comprehensive efficiency evaluation. In dynamic DEA presented by Tone 
and Tsutsui [16], the DMUs' efficiencies are assessed during several periods. However, dynamic DEA does 
not deal with DMUs' internal structures. NDEA deals with DMUs with internal. Tone and Tsutsui [16] 
proposed a network-dynamic DEA model in which the DMUs were assessed in multiple periods with internal 
interactions. Their model is based on the Slack-Based Measure (SBM) model. In other words, NDEA is one 
of the advanced DEA models for dealing with the network structure of DMUs. A wide range of NDEA 
models has been used for assessing and ranking DMUs. The NDEA can rank DMUs with network structures. 
In [33], authors proposed a multiplicative relational NDEA model in the presence of window analysis to 
capture the efficiency trends over time. However, they did not present a robust optimization model. The 
classical DEA models assume deterministic inputs and outputs. In other words, the classical methods for 
considering the uncertainty of parameters are divided into two categories: sensitivity analysis and random 
optimization. In sensitivity analysis, the uncertainty is initially ignored. Then, after solving the problem, the 
effect of uncertain data is checked [34]. 
The other study investigates the fiscal performance and efficiency of Indian states from 2009 to 2015 using a 
NDEA approach, focusing on tax mobilization and development spending. The findings reveal a positive 
correlation between efficiency scores and GDP growth as well as gross capital formation while highlighting a 
negative association between efficiency and outstanding liabilities. This emphasizes the necessity for balanced

361
governmental spending strategies to prevent future debt crises in India. This two-stage methodology allows 
for an in depth analysis of fiscal efficiency in tax and spending processes at a granular level. A significant 
negative correlation exists between outstanding liabilities and efficiency scores, highlighting fiscal 
sustainability concerns. Finally, the study underscores the importance of effective financial planning, 
combining tax financing and quality expenditure management for sustainable growth [35]. 
The potential DEA has for being used as a useful instrument in tackling sustainability concerns in SCM. The 
results highlight the growing trends in the field and emphasize the role of the conceptual approaches that 
address the tensions between business performance and excellence in sustainable practices. The Key points 
of this paper are:  
I. About 352 articles focusing on the DEA in SCM, and the number of citations reached 10,445, illustrating 
the increasing importance of the field. The majority of the analyzed papers were published in Business 
Economics and Engineering, which proves the best behavior of the literature in the further investigation of 
the context. 
II. The majority of citations for DEA in SCM relate to incorporating neural networks and multi-criteria 
decision-making for green supplier selection. 
III. As one would expect, there has been a surge of demand for effective supply chain sustainability in the most 
recent years, where DEA has been recognized as one of the key tools in measuring sustainability [36].  
In past research, organizational performance assessment primarily focused on economic and financial 
indicators; however, a shift has emerged toward recognizing essential cognitive and social parameters that 
meet scientific standards, driven by growing human needs. The Balanced Scorecard (BSC) framework seeks 
to balance past-oriented financial metrics with future-oriented dimensions such as customer processes, 
learning and growth, and the development of human and social capital. DEA serves as a non-parametric 
method for measuring efficiency across similar units with different inputs and outputs, with a two-level DEA 
approach applicable for classifying inputs and using common weights in cases of multiple similar variables. 
This paper aims to utilize the BSC for designing performance assessment indicators and the two-level DEA 
for effective measurement, acknowledging the expansion and diversification of inputs in today's complex 
social systems [37]. Measuring the performance of production systems is a vital management function, 
effectively addressed by the BSC framework, which offers a structured visualization of key performance 
indicators across financial, customer, internal process, and learning and growth dimensions. 
In contrast, conventional DEA methods assess systems as a whole, failing to evaluate individual processes. 
This paper introduces an integrated BSC-DEA methodology that facilitates multi-criteria performance 
evaluation across various project stages. The combined BSC-DEA model employs cards to categorize input 
and output metrics linked to BSC perspectives, allowing for efficiency decomposition to pinpoint specific 
processes that contribute to systemic inefficiencies and guide future improvements. The authors illustrate this 
integrated BSC-DEA framework through a case study of six banking branches, demonstrating its ability to 
provide a more comprehensive assessment of organizational performance compared to traditional methods 
[38]. 
The other model focuses on a two-warehouse system for perishable items, employing minimal total cost 
strategies during COVID-19 lockdowns. This study presents an inventory model designed to minimize costs 
in a two-warehouse system while accounting for deterioration rates influenced by time and disruptions in 
demand due to the COVID-19 lockdown. Two distinct scenarios are analyzed: one with stock depletion after 
lockdown and another with depletion during lockdown. The study incorporates time-dependent demand and 
deterioration rates represented using weibull distributions. Utilizing a Last In, First Out (LIFO) policy helps 
reduce overall holding costs in the higher-cost rented warehouse. The research includes sensitivity analysis, 
indicating that various factors, such as demand fluctuations and warehouse conditions, significantly impact 
costs. Numerical examples demonstrate practical applications of the model, confirming the higher total costs

associated with longer lockdown periods. The findings advocate for enhanced capacity and strategic use of 
owned warehouses to minimize holding and deterioration costs effectively [39].  
2.4|Research Gap 
To the best of our knowledge, there is no article to evaluate sustainable supply chains using ML for Clustering 
and two-stage NDEA in the presence of undesirable outputs especially in petrochemical industries in Iran. 
This paper presents an approach to measure sustainability using both desirable and undesirable outputs. One 
of the knowledge gap insights in this article is the importance of grouping similar industrial units in on cluster 
and making plans to develop inefficient units to reach the most efficient unit of each cluster.  
One of the key challenges in comparing relative efficiency using traditional NDEA lies in benchmarking the 
performance of all units against the most efficient units on the efficiency frontier. This approach is logically 
flawed, as it fails to account for the varying capacities and specific capabilities of petrochemical units in Iran. 
A more effective strategy involves classifying homogeneous units into specific clusters, where the units share 
similar input and output volumes. Moreover, the application of ML in the clustering of supply chains remains 
underexplored. This article addresses this gap by proposing a method for selecting the most appropriate 
algorithm based on the dataset under investigation and offers a framework for comparing algorithms to 
identify the optimal one based on clustering quality. Consequently, this research not only demonstrates the 
process of selecting the best algorithm but also highlights the impact of using ML in sustainable supply chain 
clustering. Additionally, this approach offers valuable insights for planning the developmental strategies of 
industrial units, and our proposed hybrid method using ML and NDEA can be implemented for various 
industries; this article has examined a case study for the proposed method. 
3|Proposed Method 
In summary, to introduce our proposed approach, the following steps are taken: In Step 1, DMUs are clustered 
using three distinct algorithms within the realm of ML. In Step 2, The clustering outputs of three different 
algorithms were compared using clustering quality evaluation indicators, resulting in the identification of the 
best clustering algorithm for the dataset of our case study. In Step 3, evaluate the efficiency of the DMUs in 
each cluster separately and identify the most efficient unit in the cluster. In Step 4, the results from the previous 
step were compared with the conventional DEA method. In the last step, the results showed that assessing 
the efficiency among homogeneous DMUs in each cluster can lead inefficient DMUs to plan achievable 
development plans. These steps are presented below: 
Step 1. DMUs are clustered using three distinct ML algorithms: by using Python programming language, 
three different algorithms will be implemented on the dataset of our case study. 
Step 2. Selecting the best clustering method: by comparing the results of three clustering algorithms with 
each other, in this step, the clustering quality of the three different algorithms will be compared based on 
mathematical scores with each other. 
Step 3. Calculating efficiency in each cluster by NDEA model: evaluating the efficiency of DMUs in each 
cluster separately and identifying the most efficient DMU in each cluster as the best unit. 
Step 4. Comparing the proposed method with the traditional NDEA approach: all results from our proposed 
method based on two-stage NDEA will compared with traditional NDEA, which is calculating efficiency 
between all DMUs without clustering.  
Step 5. Discussion and development planning: analysis and discussion is the final step. In this step, the 
efficient unit will be considered as a target for other units in each cluster. 
3.1|Two-Stage NDEA 
In the initial DEA, the internal structures of DMUs are ignored. The NDEA assesses the efficiency of the

4|Case Study: Petrochemical Sustainable Supply Chain in Iran 
The oil and gas industry is one of the most crucial for Iran. It is one of the largest producers of oil and natural 
gas in the world and has access to vast reserves of both resources. Moreover, the petrochemical industry in 
the country is also significant. Among the products that the industry manufactures are plastics, fertilizers, and 
various chemicals; they find application in agriculture, manufacturing, and construction. Several plants are 
producing these goods in Iran, which belong to both governmental and private companies. For the 
petrochemical industry in Iran, SCM is an essential component since it integrates sourcing, production, and 
the movement of raw materials and finished products at various levels. Iran's petrochemical sector produces 
a wide array of products, and sourcing and transportation to numerous countries is only possible with the 
coordination brought by acquiring the necessary resources and moving products to the foreign market. 
Finally, effective SCM is essential for the success of the petrochemical industry in Iran. By optimizing 
processes, reducing costs, and improving efficiency, companies can enhance their competitiveness and 
contribute to the growth and development of the industry as a whole. 
In a two-stage supply chain model in the petrochemical industry, with the first stage being extraction and the 
second stage being refining, incorporating sustainability practices is crucial for ensuring environmental 
responsibility, social impact, and economic viability. 
Extraction stage 
In the extraction phase, companies can implement sustainable practices such as B. Minimizing environmental 
impact, reducing water consumption, and handling waste responsibilities. This may include using advanced 
drilling and production technologies, implementing water recycling systems, and adhering to strict 
environmental regulations. Companies can work with local communities near mining sites to ensure their

operations are socially responsible and benefit local communities. This may include providing employment 
opportunities, supporting local infrastructure development, and advising community members on 
environmental and social issues. In addition to the mentioned cases, controlling the HSE unit to increase the 
safety of personnel is another necessary measure in the field of social responsibilities in the criteria of 
sustainability. 
Refining stage 
In the refining phase, DMUs can focus on improving energy efficiency and reducing greenhouse gas 
emissions. This may include investing in energy-efficient technologies, optimizing production processes and 
using renewable energy sources where possible. Refineries can also implement waste management practices 
to minimize waste generation, recycle materials, and safely dispose of hazardous waste. This can help reduce 
environmental impact and comply with regulatory requirements. By integrating sustainability principles into 
both the extraction and refining phases of the petrochemical supply chain, companies can reduce their 
environmental footprint, increase their social impact and improve their long-term profitability. This approach 
not only benefits the company but also contributes to the overall sustainability of the petrochemical industry 
in Iran. 
4.1|Two-Stage Sustainable Supply Chain in the Petrochemical Industry 
Crude oil is extracted from the oil fields in the first stage, known as the extraction phase, and is then imported 
into the refinery along with other inputs deemed stable in the second stage. The desired and undesirable 
outputs from the second level are separated in this section, and each DMU a distinct two-stage supply chain 
that is defined in this paper then attempts to increase the desirable output and decrease the undesirable output. 
The efficiency calculated with the NDEA model is based on the sustainability aspect, which is considered in 
inputs and outputs during the supply chains. 
It is important to highlight that the intended outcomes encompass all usable petroleum derivatives upon 
completion of the process. This includes final products that are consumable without further processing, such 
as chemical fertilizers, polyethylene, acids, chemical solvents, and consumable feed. These products are 
ultimately intended for consumption by end consumers or secondary industrial entities, particularly in sectors 
such as pharmaceuticals. It is essential to consider the diverse range of output products from petrochemical 
facilities to ensure comparability, with their value expressed in terms of revenue from product sales in dollar 
units as the desired outcome. Undesirable output refers to oil waste that is not suitable for consumption and 
must be reintroduced into the refining process for re-refining or disposed of as waste outside of the cycle. 
The aim is to minimize these occurrences in subsequent cycles; in addition to considering inputs of a cost 
nature, the outputs are also evaluated in terms of dollar units within the dataset and supply chain framework. 
The dataset of the case study in this paper is taken from Samane Andishmand Pegah (SAP) software. SAP is 
a knowledge-based company in the fields of consulting, planning, and production of Enterprise Resource 
Planning (ERP) software. SAP wishes to evaluate and forecast the sustainability of supply chains of the 
industries. One of the company's areas of operation includes the oil and petrochemical industries. As is seen 
in Fig. 5, each supply chain has two stages: extraction and Refinery. The inputs and outputs of the supply 
chain are selected based on sustainability concepts and a literature review, which is available in Table 7 in the 
Appendix section. These are the factors for assessing efficiency with a sustainability approach. The inputs and 
outputs of each stage are defined as follows: 
Extraction stage: in the extraction stage, we define the inputs and outputs of oil, which are located in 28 
locations in Iran. 
Inputs 
I. Cost of workforce safety (Social responsibility criterion): Costs that are paid to protect and secure workers 
in the extraction phase (Million $). 
II. Personnel salary cost (Economic criterion): Salaries paid for personnel in the extraction stage (Million $).

4.2|Clustering DMUs with Machine Learning 
ML plays a crucial role in SCM-related criteria. First, ML can describe the non-linear relationship while 
traditional methods do not, for the training model of ML better describes how the output (y) changes with 
input (x). Second, ML is able to deal with unstructured data sets where traditional models fail. For instance, a 
traditional linear regression model requires a sample size larger than the number of features in the data sets. 
However, a small company usually fails to gain enough sample size for their SC records. Third, ML and its 
core constructs are suitable for better SCM performance prediction. If taking the strengths of unsupervised 
learning, supervised learning, and reinforcement learning into consideration, ML is extremely effective in 
continually seeking the key factors most affecting SC performance. Fourth, with the help of visual pattern 
recognition across an SC network, ML is able to explore a lot of potential applications in the maintenance of 
physical assets and physical inspection. According to Fig. 2 and based on the literature review, the ML 
algorithms frequently used in SCM and figure out the SCM activities most suitable for ML. We found a gap 
that addressed the lack of use of ML algorithms for clustering DMUs in SCM and SSCM in particular. Some 
of the applicable use cases of ML in SCM have been listed below: 
I. 
Demand forecasting: ML algorithms can analyze historical sales data, market trends, and other relevant 
factors to predict future demand for products. This helps companies optimize their inventory levels and 
reduce stockouts or overstock situations. 
II. 
Inventory management: ML can be used to optimize inventory levels by analyzing various factors such as 
lead times, demand variability, and supplier reliability. This helps in minimizing holding costs while ensuring 
product availability. 
III. 
Supplier management: ML can analyze supplier performance data to identify patterns and predict potential 
issues such as late deliveries or quality problems. This can help in improving supplier relationships and 
reducing supply chain disruptions. 
IV. 
Transportation optimization: ML algorithms can analyze transportation data to optimize route planning, 
load scheduling, and carrier selection. This can lead to cost savings and improved delivery performance. 
V. 
Warehouse management: ML can be used to optimize warehouse layouts, pick paths, and storage locations, 
leading to more efficient operations and reduced labor costs. 
VI. 
Risk management: ML can analyze various factors such as geopolitical events, natural disasters, and market 
trends to predict and mitigate supply chain risks. 
In a broader context, ML holds the potential to enhance the decision-making capabilities of SCM 
practitioners, optimize operational processes, and drive cost-reduction initiatives. The present study aims to 
delve into the utilization of ML techniques, specifically in the clustering of decision units within the realm of 
SCM. Python is used for all programming tasks in this section to evaluate and compare the ML algorithms. 
Following this, three ML algorithms utilized in this study will be introduced, and the results obtained from 
them will be analyzed. 
K-means 
K-means is an unsupervised clustering method. This method is able to divide the data into k clusters which 
would decrease the square errors of each group. K-means is time-and-cost-efficient with its low 
computational complexity, but the K needs to be preset in advance, which requires prior knowledge of the 
data sets. What is worse, K-means is prone to being affected by outliers and noise. We should add that K-
means works with the concept of maximum similarity between inputs and outputs of DMUs [41]. 
Hierarchial clustering 
Among the unsupervised machine methods is a top-down approach. In this method, each cluster may be 
placed in more than one cluster because the subset of the larger cluster itself may be at another level of 
distance. One of the most important points in the implementation of this method is the selection of
18:
Clustering with machine learning and using NDEA in development planning: A case study in… 
372
amount of intra-cluster dispersion (That is, the greatest similarity between members) and the maximum 
amount of inter-cluster dispersion (That is, the greatest difference between other clusters) to be If the number 
of data dimensions is more than one, it is necessary to calculate the total dispersion between the dimensions 
under the title of total dispersion. In the following, the three evaluation indicators used in this research will 
be examined: 
I. Silhouette score: the silhouette score is an indicator used for assessing clustering quality in datasets. It 
determines the similarity of an object to its cluster in comparison to other clusters. The scale of this indicator 
is [-1, 1]. A high score indicates good matching within the cluster but poor matching with neighboring 
clusters, while a low score suggests the opposite. A higher silhouette score indicates more distinct clusters, 
making it a valuable tool for evaluating clustering algorithms and determining the ideal number of clusters 
for a dataset. 
II. Davies-Bouldin score: To calculate this indicator, it is necessary to calculate the two measures of dispersion 
size and dissimilarity between clusters. According to this criterion, the more dispersion there is inside the 
clusters, the greater its value, and the smaller the distance between two clusters, the greater its value. In fact, 
the average value of this criterion will be the maximum ratio of dispersion within to dispersion between 
clusters, and the lower the value, the higher the quality of the clustering operation. 
III. Calinski-Harabasz score: It is the ratio of variance to the ratio between intra-cluster dispersion and inter-
cluster dispersion. In other words, it is also called Variance Ratio Criterion (VRC). 
At the end of this section, first, the results of clustering by the three introduced algorithms will be displayed. 
Then, the results obtained based on three evaluation indicators will be examined. In the next step, after 
determining the best clustering algorithm for the dataset of the case study, the relative efficiency of DMUs 
placed in each cluster based on the network data envelopment model will be calculated and compared with 
the non-clustering state. In the realm of data science, a pivotal procedure undertaken by experts involves the 
meticulous selection of an optimal clustering algorithm through visual scrutiny of the data space. By closely 
examining the scatter plots and data structures, the range of software implementation choices is refined to 
attain the most effective solution. 
Nevertheless, in contemporary scenarios marked by escalating data dimensions, the visualization of a graph 
incorporating multiple features often transcends the conventional two or three-dimensional space, rendering 
the depiction of a dataset infeasible. In such instances, the employment of dimensionality reduction 
techniques like Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is recommended. It is imperative to note that the 
application of this solution necessitates tailored programming within the database to align with its distinctive 
variables and attributes. 
The data of 28 two-level supply chains in petrochemical industries has been collected for 90 time periods. 
The best clustering quality in these 90 time periods belongs to the period in which the quality assessment 
indices have the best values among all periods. The Table 2, the data of the best period between 90 time series, 
in addition to the results of three ML clustering algorithms, has been illustrated. According to the findings 
presented in the first three columns of Table 2, the outcomes of the three ML subset algorithms have been 
successfully implemented. The variation in cluster numbering can be attributed to the nature of clustering 
falling within the domain of unsupervised learning, where there are no predefined labels to delineate units 
within this domain. In Table 2, at each stage, three cost data points with a focus on sustainability indicators 
are regarded as significant. The revenue generated from light oil serves as an intermediary factor between 
stages 1 and 2, denoted in millions of dollars, while the income of the industrial unit, acquired as the desirable 
output at the conclusion of stage 2, is placed alongside the undesirable output. To ensure comparability with 
the quantity of desirable outputs, predominantly oil and gas products, two additional columns have been 
included based on tonnage. It is important to note that these tonnage columns are excluded from the 
calculations of relative efficiency.

The inquiry arises as to why clustering based on increased similarities among units will transpire? The research 
underscores the utilization of clustering as a focal point. This study aims to render inefficient DMUs 
comprehensible at a more accessible level to enhance and advance their developmental indicators. Through 
this approach, each unit can strategize its industrial unit development plan based on the best DMU in each 
cluster and explain its development strategies. Efficient DMUs in each cluster can make their development 
plan with a target of the most efficient DMU between all efficiency calculations. Finally, all the inefficient 
DMUs are able to plan a more achievable development plan in relation to the volume of production and the 
amount of inputs and outputs.

4.3|Assessing Efficiency with NDEA 
In this section, efficiency calculations were conducted using the equations outlined in Figs. 3 and 4, employing 
NDEA within the GAMS software. The efficiency results presented in Fig. 7 encompass all petrochemical 
industrial DMUs in Iran without factoring in clustering at first. Within Fig. 7, under the assumption of CRS , 
efficiency equations were derived in two scenarios: one without undesirable output and the other 
incorporating undesirable output. The comparison of total efficiency across these equations was undertaken 
through two distinct approaches: 
I. Multiplicative method: In this case, the total efficiency is equal to the multiplication of the efficiency of stage 
1 by stage 2. In this method, in order to ensure the stability of the obtained values, all performance values 
will be calculated on the condition that the values calculated in the previous stage are constant. 
II. Weighted average method: In this method, the total efficiency is equal to the weighted average of the 
efficiency of stage 1 and stage 2. The weight of each stage is equal to the impact of the inputs. It should be 
noted that the impact of each stage can be adjusted by managers based on the importance of each stage in 
each period that determines the outputs.

Having established the impact of clustering on efficiency calculations, the next step involves identifying the 
most efficient unit within each cluster. In the proposed methodology, efficiency assessments are conducted 
for each cluster in accordance with the proposed NDEA two-stage SSC. The most efficient unit in each 
cluster is regarded as a benchmark for the other inefficient units. This approach provides these units with a 
more attainable goal compared to efficiency calculations conducted without clustering across all 28 units. 
The most efficient units within each cluster will use the top-performing unit among the 28 as a reference 
point for developing sustainability-based production indicators. Overall, this strategy facilitates a more 
realistic and achievable development plan for the industrial units. Furthermore, this methodology can be 
applied across various supply chains to implement a phased development policy, allowing for incremental 
progress toward enhanced efficiency and sustainability. 
The current review of research within the field of data science emphasizes the comparison of execution times 
among various algorithms as a critical component. However, it is important to note that this study primarily 
focuses on the quality of clustering and the identification of optimal separation between units. Given the 
nature of the dataset employed, the computational time required for clustering is relatively minimal and does 
not serve as a determining factor for identifying the most effective clustering method. Consequently, the 
aspect of banding is not addressed in this analysis. Nevertheless, the comparison of execution time and the 
complexity of the Python code implemented in this research is presented in Table 6. 
Table 6. The time and space complexity of algorithm comparison. 
 
 
 
For example, the time complexity of the my k-means clustering function is primarily determined by the K-
Means clustering algorithm, which has a time complexity of O(n * k * d * i), where n is the number of data 
points, k is the number of clusters, d is the number of dimensions, and i is the number of iterations. The 
storage of the input data, the K-Means model, the cluster labels, and the output results determines the space 
complexity of the function. The input data is stored as a numpy array, which requires O(n * d) space. The K-
Means model also requires space for storing the cluster centers and labels, which is typically O(k * d). The 
cluster labels and output results are stored in lists, which require additional space. Overall, the space 
complexity can be considered as O(n*d + k*d + n). Analysis of the DEC showed that overall, the time 
complexity of the DEC_clustering function is dominated by the training of the autoencoder model and the 
clustering of the encoded data, which are both O(n) or O(n2) depending on the specific parameters of the

6|Conclusion and Managerial Implications 
The utilization of data science in enhancing and interpreting datasets stands as a crucial element in gaining a 
competitive edge within the industry, with these datasets being recognized as implicit assets within 
organizational contexts. The current research study placed particular emphasis on the role of data science in 
the domain of SCM as part of the research methodology, specifically delving into the utilization of ML 
algorithms in the domain of clustering, a form of unsupervised learning. In examining contemporary 
algorithms featured in international articles and publications, this paper explored the significance of such 
reviews in prior research endeavors concerning the assessment of relative efficiency and sustainability within 
the supply chain. This investigation aimed to chart a research path in the realm of enhancing supply chain 
efficiency through the application of NDEA within a data science framework. 
The study demonstrated that the optimal dividing of DMUs into clusters based on maximum similarity can 
offer a more effective practical solution compared to conventional DEA methods for assessing relative 
efficiency. By clustering DMUs, inefficient units within each cluster were brought closer to improve efficiency 
by aligning them with the most efficient unit in their respective cluster. This approach may serve as a viable 
strategy for short-term goal-setting within industrial units to strive towards achieving the efficiency levels of 
the leading unit in their cluster. Conversely, while relative efficiency has been applied to all DMUs irrespective 
of clustering, the efficient units within each cluster can aim to achieve the performance metrics of the most 
efficient unit among the 28 industrial units. This approach allows for the utilization of the methodology 
proposed in this article to conduct calculations and chart a path for operational enhancement across all units. 
The findings indicated that through clustering, the inefficiency level of each decision unit decreased, 
highlighting the necessity for units within each cluster to emulate the performance of the most efficient unit 
in their respective cluster to attain relative efficiency. It is important to note that by employing DEA in a 
multiplicative form and giving precedence to stage 2, a significant portion of the weights in the equation were 
assigned to optimize the efficiency of stage 2. Clustering and introducing a hierarchical improvement strategy 
can motivate industrial units to engage in continuous enhancement and advancement, a notion supported by 
the simulation results. In a separate study comparing efficiency calculations with and without undesirable 
outputs, the findings revealed that considering undesirable outputs as desirable outputs through a linear 
transformation resulted in increased total efficiency and stage 2 efficiency across all scenarios compared to 
calculations without undesirable outputs. This method underscores the beneficial impact of incorporating this 
factor in enhancing the precision of relative efficiency evaluations. Such an approach fosters ongoing 
improvement and incremental progress within industries, reflecting a key aspect of sustainable development 
in management principles. 
For future research and the continuation of the present study, in addition to exploring modeling techniques 
such as Dynamic Network Data Envelopment Analysis (DNDEA) with a sustainability approach, further 
investigation into various clustering methods, including clustering using DEA, could be beneficial using the 
existing dataset. The DEA-based clustering method involves utilizing piecewise production functions derived 
from the DEA approach to cluster data based on input and output characteristics. This approach not only 
assigns DMUs to specific clusters but also considers the type of production function characteristic of each 
cluster. This method can be instrumental for managerial decision-making, particularly for identifying 
necessary adjustments in input-output resource combinations to position units within desirable clusters. On 
the other hand, The design of a Closed-Loop Supply Chain (CLSC) amid uncertainty and partial disruptions 
poses significant challenges. CLSC aims to integrate reverse logistics into traditional supply chains, promoting 
sustainability and environmental friendliness. However, such uncertainties can hinder the efficiency and 
reliability of CLSCs. To tackle these issues, a chance constraint is proposed, enabling the incorporation of
26:
Clustering with machine learning and using NDEA in development planning: A case study in… 
380
probabilistic uncertainties into decision-making processes. The objective is to create a robust CLSC model 
that adeptly manages uncertain factors like demand, return rates, and product quality. The study employs the 
Markowitz approach to address uncertainty in the objective function by blending the mean with the standard 
deviation coefficient. Findings indicate that integrating uncertainty into the model enhances profitability 
compared to a deterministic approach, as the uncertain model demonstrates greater responsiveness to 
fluctuating demands and effectively manages confidence inventory dynamics. While strategic decisions 
regarding the number of production, distribution, and destruction facilities remain largely unchanged, the 
capacity of destruction centers in the uncertain model is slightly reduced due to varying product quality. 
Overall, the inclusion of uncertainty improves the model's transparency and supports better decision-making, 
leading to increased profitability through enhanced demand responsiveness and inventory management [44]. 
Furthermore, examining the impact of past efficiency levels on future efficiency could involve utilizing the 
DNDEA model to analyze two-stage supply chains. This model can account for feedback loops involving 
the return of waste to the refining cycle and incorporate additional factors into the calculation of relative 
efficiency. This expanded analysis could provide valuable insights into optimizing supply chain operations 
and enhance overall efficiency in a dynamic environment. 
Another idea is about the efficiency of Industry 4.0 implementations, which utilize advanced computerized 
systems by employing both classical and fuzzy DEA models. Recognizing the uncertainty in the required data, 
the authors introduce fuzzy DEA to provide a more accurate evaluation of productivity, allowing for different 
units of measurement and independence from analytical production functions. This study is notable as the 
first quantitative analysis of industry 4.0's impact on productivity, demonstrating how fuzzy factors can 
influence decision-making. A practical application in a home appliances manufacturing company further 
illustrates the advantages of fuzzy DEA over traditional methods in assessing the effects of Industry 4.0 on 
machine productivity [45]. 
Author Contributaion 
The first author (Sina Sayardoost): this author had the primary role in idea generation, research design, data 
collection, formal analysis, and article writing. He has also participated in providing supplementary cases as 
one of the main authors. The second author (Keikhosro Yakideh): responsible and corresponding author, 
involved in formal analysis, data verification and review, project planning, and reviewing. The third author 
(Mahmoud Moradi) contributed to formal analysis for the research and review. The fourth author (Mostafa 
Ebrahimpour) was involved in writing, reviewing, and editing the article. All authors have read and approved 
the manuscript. Only individuals who contributed significantly to the reported work are credited as authors. 
Data Availability 
The dataset of the case study in this paper is taken from Samane Andishmand Pegah software. Samane 
Andishmand Pegah (SAP) is a knowledge-based company in the fields of consulting, planning and production 
of ERP software. The dataset which is used in this paper was about the supply chain of petrochemical 
industries and is available at the following link: (https://b2n.ir/sinasayardoost_Dataset).
